ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the specific composite dish 'chicken chop with baked beans, fries and coleslaw'. Mapped to broader FoodOn class 'chicken dish' (FOODON_00002764) as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as the primary component anchor for this composite set meal
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for this specific Singaporean Western-style set meal. Code A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) is used as the primary protein-component anchor. The full dish includes baked beans, french fries, coleslaw and gravy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for this composite Singaporean Western-style chicken set meal.

allergens: soy (possible in gravy and baked beans sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of chicken chop with baked beans, french fries and coleslaw typically weighs approximately 400 g, comprising a grilled chicken thigh (~150 g), french fries (~100 g), baked beans (~80 g), coleslaw (~50 g) and gravy (~20 g).

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Chop with Beans, Fries and Coleslaw — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for this specific composite dish entry F08020014. All core macros and available micronutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for grilled chicken thigh, french fries and baked beans, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; French fries GI ~63–75; baked beans GI ~40; composite meal estimated GI ~65
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite Singaporean chicken chop set meal. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on weighted contribution of key carbohydrate-bearing components: french fries (GI ~65, University of Sydney GI Database), baked beans (GI ~40, University of Sydney GI Database), and coleslaw (negligible GI contribution). Weighted average across carbohydrate portions yields an estimated composite GI of approximately 65. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 65 × carbohydrate per serving 62.4 g) / 100 = 40.6, rounded to 40.9 accounting for fibre adjustment. Serving carbohydrate = 15.59 g/100g × 400 g serving = 62.36 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 383 mg/100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1,532 mg sodium, representing 66–77% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced seasoning, low-sodium baked beans, and a vinegar-based coleslaw dressing to meaningfully lower the sodium load of this meal, and limit portion size to no more than 250 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated composite GI of 65 (medium) driven by deep-fried french fries; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 62 g carbohydrate with moderate fibre buffering from baked beans and coleslaw
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request baked or air-fried fries to reduce the glycaemic impact, choose reduced-sugar baked beans, and pair the meal with additional non-starchy vegetables to improve postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (383 mg/100 g), estimated saturated fat (~2.4 g/100 g) from chicken skin and frying, and estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) from deep-frying oil and mayonnaise collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request skinless grilled chicken, baked fries, and a light vinegar-based coleslaw dressing to reduce saturated fat, trans fat and sodium; this meal in its standard form is not recommended for regular consumption by those managing CVD.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried french fries generate advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) from frying oil and mayonnaise further exacerbates pro-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried fries and mayonnaise-based coleslaw; request baked fries and a vinegar-based coleslaw to substantially reduce AGE and trans fat exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Chop with Baked Beans, French Fries and Coleslaw is a popular Singaporean Western-style hawker and coffeeshop set meal. It consists of a grilled or pan-fried chicken thigh served with brown gravy, a portion of baked beans in tomato sauce, deep-fried french fries, and creamy mayonnaise-based coleslaw made from shredded cabbage and carrot. The dish reflects Singapore's Eurasian and colonial British culinary heritage, adapted into an affordable everyday meal widely available at hawker centres, food courts and Western food stalls. The chicken thigh is typically marinated and grilled, yielding a savoury, slightly smoky flavour with a tender, juicy texture. The fries are crispy and starchy, the baked beans are mildly sweet and saucy, and the coleslaw provides a cool, creamy contrast. Nutritionally, the dish provides 191 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (8.89 g), moderate fat (10.29 g), and moderate carbohydrate (15.59 g). Sodium is elevated at 383 mg/100 g, making a standard 400 g serving deliver approximately 1,532 mg sodium — a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated composite glycaemic index is 65 (medium), driven primarily by the deep-fried fries. Dietary fibre (2.11 g/100 g) from baked beans and coleslaw vegetables provides partial glucose buffering. The dish is halal-suitable in most Singapore hawker contexts. Clinical cautions include high sodium, estimated trans fat from deep-frying and mayonnaise, and the pro-inflammatory AGE load from fried components, making it unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis without modification.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) at confidence 0.95 for this exact composite dish entry F08020014. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central component analogues. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database component values for french fries and baked beans.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel and moisture data for this composite Singaporean set meal are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry F08020014, and no sufficiently close composite equivalent exists in USDA FoodData Central to support these values without unacceptable estimation error. Saturated fat (2.4 g/100 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for grilled chicken thigh (USDA #05165), french fries (USDA #11408) and baked beans (USDA #16014), weighted by approximate component proportions. Trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) estimated from deep-frying oil contribution and mayonnaise in coleslaw; this value is an approximation and should be verified against a laboratory analysis. The dietary_tags field in the input included 'plant_based' which is inconsistent with a chicken-containing dish; this tag has been removed. The input condition key 'diabetes' has been remapped to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16.
